label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what we can infer from the Toy Fair 2014 based on the article. First, I need to go through the article again to pick out the key points. The article mentions that the 2014 Toy Fair was the biggest in its history, with over 1,000 companies. The trend this year was "Larger Than Life," meaning big toys with big play value. Adrienne Appell says that in 2014, everything is really big and out there. So that supports the idea that big toys are popular. Then there's a part about a push towards education with STEAM (science, tech, engineering, art, math), including word games and high-tech games. But it also says that they saw some super cool, high-tech games. Appell mentions technology influencing toys, like 3D games and robots. But does that mean robots are the favorite? The article says "Larger Than Life" was a favorite, which is about size. The robots are part of the tech trend, but the question is about what we can infer. Looking at the options: A) Toys are becoming more educational than fun. The article says there's a push towards education, but it doesn't say they are becoming more educational than fun. The fair is described as "all fun and games," but also includes educational aspects. So maybe it's a balance, not necessarily more educational than fun. B) Robots are great favorites of buyers. The article mentions robots as part of the tech trend, but there's no specific mention that they were the favorites of buyers. The "Larger Than Life" trend was a favorite, which is about size. C) Big toys will be popular this year. The article explicitly says that "Larger Than Life" was a favorite and the trend is about big toys. Also, Appell states "everything is really big and really out there." So this seems like a strong candidate. D) Simple card games will disappear on the market.
